policy_statement: Provide technical support to local governments/public entities to develop and implement clean energy projects.
mechanism_description: NYSERDA will work with community stakeholders to provide technical support that helps local governments and related public entities develop and implement clean energy projects, enabling more clean energy deployment that can displace fossil generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Technical assistance/capacity building → ↑ clean energy project deployment → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Public agencies and local governments receive expert support (planning, engineering scoping, procurement, financing, interconnection/permitting navigation) that reduces non-technical barriers to implementing renewable energy, storage, and other clean energy projects. (2) Mediating physical process: Greater installed capacity and utilization of low/zero-carbon energy increases the share of demand met without combustion, thereby reducing combustion of coal/oil/gas in the marginal and average supply mix. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and co-pollutant) emissions from electricity/energy supply and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased clean energy project deployment that displaces fossil fuel generation, leading to lower GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Enabling services (technical assistance) lead to more clean energy projects being deployed, which displace fossil generation and reduce GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The principal intended and physical outcome is emissions reduction via fuel switching in energy supply, not primarily reducing energy use (Resource Efficiency), managing climate hazards (Adaptation), or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Although technical assistance is an “enabling” action and could sometimes support efficiency or resilience projects, this canonical mechanism is explicitly tied to clean energy deployment and fossil displacement; therefore the dominant effect remains mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that uses this mechanism—technical/project-development support explicitly aimed at clean energy deployment—has its largest climate effect through increased low/zero-carbon supply replacing fossil supply, yielding emissions reductions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the supported projects are solar, wind, storage, geothermal, clean district energy, etc., and the magnitude of reductions depend on project specifics and grid mix; those secondary details must be assigned at the individual policy/project evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “clean energy” assistance is used mainly for non-displacing actions (e.g., administrative planning with no deployment, or projects that increase supply without reducing fossil output due to curtailment), realized mitigation may be smaller; however the mechanism’s dominant intended pathway is still fossil displacement via added clean generation.
key_indicators: "Provide technical support"; "NYSERDA should work with community stakeholders"; "help local governments and related public entities develop and implement clean energy projects"; "clean energy projects"
co_benefits: Potential air quality/public health improvements from reduced combustion; local economic development/workforce benefits; improved energy affordability/resilience (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Technical assistance is not a perfect fit for “incentive-program” (it’s non-financial support), but among the allowed instrument types it aligns best with a programmatic support intervention (as opposed to regulation/standards/investment). If a dedicated “technical assistance” type existed, it would map there.
